Who I Am

A Millennial with a Dream,
a Scientist with a Purpose

I grew up in Manning, SC, raised by a single mother, my grandparents, and the village it takes. I was shaped by church, community, and people who believed in me before I fully believed in myself. By high school I was a leader in band, nominated for national youth leadership conferences, and earning my place as a leader in my own right.

I attended Bethune-Cookman University, The H[BCU], where I studied Biology with a pre-med focus. A summer program at Duke University’s School of Medicine gave me my first real exposure to medicine and clinical research. But it was two summers at the University of Florida’s ReTOOL Program (focused on molecular and translational research in prostate cancer) that ignited my passion for cancer science. That experience sparked a new world and made me a researcher.

I went on to earn my M.S. in Integrative Biology from Kennesaw State University and my Ph.D. in Medical Sciences: Cancer Biology from the University of Florida. I am now a Postdoctoral Fellow at Henry Ford Health, where I investigate epigenetic regulation in cancer and the tumor microenvironment.

Beyond the bench, I organize educational symposia, develop cancer biology curricula for high school students, and mentor the next generation, especially students of color. My goal: expose as many people as possible to the possibility of a life in science, as early as possible.

What I Study

Research Program

Health disparities are a fundamental barrier to equitable cancer care. My work bridges molecular mechanisms and population-level inequities — asking not just how cancer grows, but who is left behind by our current treatments.

01

Epigenetic Regulation in Cancer

Investigating how histone dynamics and chromatin remodeling complexes are co-opted by tumors to drive oncogenic gene expression programs, sustain malignant growth, and resist therapy. A core focus is understanding how epigenetic dysregulation shapes the tumor microenvironment and immune evasion.

02

Social Determinants of Health & Cancer Disparities

Examining how social determinants of health — including ancestry, socioeconomic status, structural racism, and access to care — interact with tumor biology to compound disparities in cancer outcomes. This arm of my work asks why certain communities bear a disproportionate burden of aggressive disease and poor therapeutic response, and how we close that gap through equitable research design and precision medicine strategies built for everyone.

03

Molecular & Genomic Instability in Cancer

Investigating how transcriptional programs arising from chromosomal instability rewire tumor biology — shaping the tumor microenvironment, driving immune evasion, and dictating therapeutic response. By linking genomic instability to downstream signaling and immune crosstalk, this work aims to identify new vulnerabilities that can be exploited to improve responses to targeted therapies and immunotherapies across diverse patient populations.
